summary: The authors secured 3rd place by implementing a high-resolution mapping rectification pipeline that separates parameter estimation from image transformation to preserve fine details. They trained an intentionally overfitted segmentation model using BCE loss to produce sharp, one-pixel-wide waveforms, then refined pixel-level predictions into floating-point coordinates via parabolic interpolation. The final submission ensembles ConvNeXt V2 and HRNet at the signal level, followed by Lead II fusion and Einthoven correction to enforce physiological constraints.
extensive_summary:
## High-Resolution Mapping Rectification
The core innovation lies in decoupling the rectification process into two stages: parameter estimation on low-resolution images and image transformation on the original high-resolution images. By scaling the homography matrix and grid coordinates to match the raw image size, the pipeline avoids quality degradation from repeated resizing. Doubling the image width to 4400 pixels significantly improved the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for the time-series ECG data while keeping the height fixed to maintain a ~1-pixel waveform thickness.

## Mask Preparation and Training Strategy
Masks were generated by drawing a strict one-pixel-wide line directly from the signal data. The authors deliberately trained an "overfitted" model with BCE loss, accepting that the model would predict thin waveforms with high confidence. Model selection relied on Dice score and CV score rather than loss value, as the raw BCE loss plateaued while the predicted waveforms remained too thick.

## Signal Segmentation Model and Augmentation
The segmentation backbone was evaluated across ConvNeXt V2, HRNet, and EfficientNet V2, with ConvNeXt V2 and HRNet selected for the final ensemble. A UnetDecoder was used for all architectures. To combat potential noise in the hidden test set and simulate unwarping distortions, the authors applied aggressive train-time augmentations including GridDropout, CoarseDropout, AddPerlinDirt, MotionBlur, Downscale, GaussianBlur, ImageCompression, GaussNoise, ISONoise, ToGray, RandomBrightnessContrast, HueSaturationValue, RandomShadow, ElasticTransform, and horizontal/vertical flips.

## Sub-pixel Signal Processing and Post-Processing
Pixel-level predictions were converted to time-series values using parabolic interpolation, which fits a local parabola to the peak and its two neighbors to compute a floating-point offset. This method was preferred over weighted centroids due to its reliability and lack of hyperparameter tuning. The pipeline concludes with Lead II fusion (averaging overlapping head regions), Einthoven correction (enforcing II=I+III with weighted importance), and TTA across original and flipped image variants.
competition_flow: Raw ECG images are rectified via a two-step high-resolution mapping process, fed into an ensembled ConvNeXt V2 and HRNet segmentation model trained with aggressive augmentation and BCE loss, then refined using parabolic interpolation and post-processed with Lead II fusion and Einthoven correction to produce the final time-series submission.
data_processing: High-resolution mapping rectification (parameter estimation on low-res, transformation on high-res)
One-pixel-wide mask generation from signal data

Aggressive train-time augmentations: GridDropout, CoarseDropout, AddPerlinDirt, MotionBlur, Downscale, GaussianBlur, ImageCompression, GaussNoise, ISONoise, ToGray, RandomBrightnessContrast, HueSaturationValue, RandomShadow, ElasticTransform, HorizontalFlip, VerticalFlip
TTA: original, horizontal flip, vertical flip, and both flips
ensembling: The final submission ensembles ConvNeXt V2 and HRNet predictions by taking a weighted average of the post-processed signals rather than raw probabilities, followed by Lead II fusion, Einthoven correction, and TTA across original and flipped variants.
insights: Separating parameter estimation from image transformation preserves high-resolution details during rectification.
Doubling the image width significantly improves SNR for time-series ECG data without excessively increasing inference memory.
Training an overfitted model with BCE loss and selecting based on Dice/CV scores yields sharper, more accurate waveform boundaries.
Parabolic interpolation reliably converts pixel-level predictions to sub-pixel precision without requiring hyperparameter tuning.
critical_findings: Widening the mask with Gaussian blur to create softer labels did not improve results, making a strict one-pixel mask preferable.
Signal-level ensembling (averaging post-processed signals) outperformed probability-level ensembling.
The model outputs very thin, sharp lines, which makes parabolic interpolation particularly effective for peak detection.
what_did_not_work: Widening the mask with Gaussian blur to create softer labels did not improve results.

other_solution_refs.description: Submission notebook referenced alongside the training code
